# Donald Trump evacuated from White House correspondents' dinner after suspected shooter apprehended

By [Audrey Courty](https://www.abc.net.au/news/audrey-courty/13735048) with wires

- [Topic:Crime](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/crime)

12h ago12 hours agoSat 25 Apr 2026 at 8:49pm

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 49 seconds.

Watch 49s

![US President Donald Trump and hife wife look shocked at a dinner table in a ballroom](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6298b1a5e0be08a9e1ea0daf08d3612c?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

The moment Donald Trump is escorted out of a gala dinner amid a security scare.

## In short:

A suspected shooter has been taken into custody after US President Donald Trump was rushed from the White House correspondents' dinner.

One Secret Service officer was shot at close range but was saved by his bulletproof vest, Mr Trump said.

## What's next?

An FBI investigation is underway, with preliminary information suggesting the 31-year-old suspect was acting alone and was a guest at the Washington Hilton where the dinner unfolded.

US President Donald Trump says a shooter has been apprehended after a security incident forced his evacuation from the White House correspondents' dinner in Washington.

Mr Trump said a man "armed with multiple weapons" charged at a security checkpoint at the Washington Hilton hotel where the event was being held.

The suspect was identified as 31-year-old Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance, California, according to AP, The Washington Post and The New York Times.

Associated Press (AP) has found social media posts that appear to match the California man. They show that he is a highly educated tutor and amateur video game developer.

A May 2025 profile photo of Cole Tomas Allen of Torrance, California, appeared to match the appearance of the man in a photo of the alleged attacker being taken into custody that was posted by Mr Trump.

The photo, posted to the social networking site LinkedIn, showed him in a cap and gown after graduating with a master's degree in computer science from California State University-Dominguez Hills.

![US President Donald Trump posted a picture of the suspected shooter on social media.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/edb82b71917acc198bbae15e6e044fe0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=560&cropW=840&xPos=262&yPos=46&width=862&height=575)

US President Donald Trump posted a picture of the suspected shooter on social media. (Truth Social: @realDonaldTrump)

"He was taken down by some very brave members of Secret Service. And they acted very quickly," Mr Trump said at a White House press conference shortly after the security scare.

The president said the suspect fired at a Secret Service agent.

> "He was shot from very close distance with a very powerful gun and the \[bulletproof\] vest did the job,"

he said.

"I just spoke to the officer and he's doing great. He's in great shape. Has very high spirits and we told him we love him and respect him."

At a separate conference, the Metropolitan Police Department Chief Jeff Carroll said the agent was taken to hospital for treatment.

![Guest take cover after hearing gunshots at the White House correspondents' dinner.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d47dd375dba915c02e4f856de7966e95?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2526&cropW=3789&xPos=0&yPos=879&width=862&height=575)

Guests take cover after hearing gunshots at the White House correspondents' dinner. (Reuters: Evan Vucci)

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 4 minutes 59 seconds.

Watch 4m 59s

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/12c37995e3ef4be0a61839fb6d4cace8?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

ABC's John Lyons documented his experience inside the White House correspondents' dinner.

Mr Carroll said the suspect, who was also taken to hospital for evaluation, "was not struck by gunfire".

He added the man was "armed with a shotgun, a handgun and multiple knives" when he stormed the security checkpoint.

US Attorney Jeanine Piro said the suspect would be charged with using a firearm during a crime of violence and assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon.

He is due to appear before a US district judge on Monday, local time.

## Suspect a 'whack job', Trump says

Authorities said the incident appeared to be isolated, though the motive and intended target remain unclear.

Mr Carroll said preliminary information suggested the suspect may have been a guest at the hotel.

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 14 seconds.

Watch 14s

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/dfa45077f567257992a5a054cc078d56?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Donald Trump also shared security footage of the suspect charging through a security checkpoint at the Washington Hotel.

An FBI official told Reuters the bureau’s counter-terrorism unit would lead the investigation.

FBI director Kash Patel said agents would examine the suspect's background "thoroughly".

"We are going to ensure that he was either a lone wolf actor or anyone else that was responsible," he said.

Asked whether he believed the suspect acted alone, Mr Trump said: "My impression is he was a lone wolf, a whack job.

"These are crazy people, and they have to be dealt with."

Play video.

Has Video Duration: 16 seconds.

Watch 16s

![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/65d965339a853d80b3bd25f2e4b7059d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Donald Trump is shielded by a security detail during the incident.

## Guests take cover as chaos unfolds

Earlier, guests had only just been seated when Secret Service agents flooded the ballroom and ordered attendees to "get down".

Mr Trump and members of his cabinet were rushed from the room after a loud, unidentified noise, according to Reuters.

Many of the roughly 2,600 attendees in the Washington Hilton hotel took cover while waiters fled to the front of the hall.

![2026-04-26T041551Z_713346124_RC2PWKAHJZ6R_RTRMADP_3_USA-TRUMP-DINNER (1)](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/afa65a51daecde6247d0bd902ee44a47?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=773&cropW=1159&xPos=0&yPos=95&width=862&height=575)

Waiters were seen fleeing to the front of the dining hall. (Reuters: Jonathan Ernst)

Dignitaries at the head table included Mr Trump, first lady Melania Trump and Vice-President JD Vance.

Both the president and vice-president were unharmed, according to an AP source.

"Quite an evening in DC, Secret Service and Law Enforcement did a fantastic job," Mr Trump wrote on Truth Social.

"The shooter has been apprehended."

![Secret service agents respond during the incident at the White House correspondents' dinner.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3d412758eeca8ae6de4ac1d162c7948d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1159&cropW=1739&xPos=161&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Secret service agents respond during the incident at the White House correspondents' dinner. (AP Photo: Tom Brenner)

![Security officials evacuate US House Speaker Mike Johnson at the White House Correspondents' dinner.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/693fb8168677a7f960e05e2175a31cb9?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1355&cropW=2033&xPos=77&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

US House Speaker Mike Johnson was evacuated by security officials. (Reuters: Jonathan Ernst)

In a subsequent post, Mr Trump said law enforcement officials requested "we leave the premises, consistent with protocol, which we will do, immediately".

"The First Lady, plus the Vice President, and all Cabinet members, are in perfect condition," he wrote.

Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said he was "pleased to hear" the US president and the first lady, along with all attendees, were safe.

"We applaud the work of the Secret Service and law enforcement agencies for their swift action," he said.

After an initial attempt to continue the correspondents' dinner, the event was scrapped for the night and will be rescheduled.

"I have spoken with all representatives in charge of the event, and we will be rescheduling within 30 days," Mr Trump said.

![Guests seen leaving the White House correspondents' dinner. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/8f7c8119581a973837c3825becf109a1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Guests were seen leaving the dinner.  (AP Photo: Allison Robbert)

White House Correspondents’ Association president Weijia Jiang said: "We will do this again."

Shortly afterwards, staff began breaking down table settings and the presidential lectern.

All officials protected by the Secret Service were evacuated and Mr Trumpreturned to the White House to hold a press conference.

Pictures also showed US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and his wife Jeanette Dousdebes Rubio at one of the West Wing entrances.

![US President Donald Trump takes questions a press conference shortly after the incident.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d3b1025a0026ea08a4ad334cd119002b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2656&cropW=3984&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

US President Donald Trump takes questions at a press conference shortly after the incident. (Reuters: Jonathan Ernst)

![2026-04-26T020502Z_1750883038_RC2QWKATS2G6_RTRMADP_3_USA-TRUMP-DINNER (1)](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4649ecf84366a926e9943a83cd624eff?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=866&cropW=1299&xPos=0&yPos=3&width=862&height=575)

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and his wife Jeanette Dousdebes Rubio wait at one of the West Wing entrances. (Reuters: Jonathan Ernst)

## Questions over venue security

A focus of the investigation is likely to be how the suspected gunman was able to smuggle weapons into the Washington Hilton hotel.

At the press conference, Mr Trump said the venue was "not a particularly secure" facility.

The Washington Hilton has long hosted the correspondents' dinner and typically remains open to regular guests during the event.

Security is generally concentrated around the ballroom, with less screening in other public areas of the hotel.

In past years, that has allowed for disruptions in lobbies and other shared spaces, including protests.

![The National Guard also responded to the security incident at the Washington Hotel.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/b5dc0894138745a886c94267b971ddc8?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2688&cropW=4032&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The National Guard also responded to the security incident at the Washington Hilton hotel. (AP Photo: Mark Schiefelbein)

Asked whether the incident represented a failure of security, Mr Carroll said a "multi-layered" approach had been in place.

"We had officers on the outside from the Metropolitan Police Department along with the escort," he said.

"The inside portion of any presidential site is handled by the Secret Service and law enforcement partners."

He said the checkpoint where the suspect was confronted was designed to intercept hotel guests.

"When he reached that checkpoint, Secret Service agents and law enforcement engaged him immediately," Mr Carroll said.

"They were able to bring him into custody and prevent anyone from being harmed."

Members of the National Guard took up position inside the building as people were allowed to leave but not immediately re-enter.

It was the third time since 2024 that the US president had been under threat by an attacker in his immediate vicinity.

In 1981, US President Ronald Reagan was shot by John Hinckley Jr outside the Hilton hotel — prompting redesigns of the property that increased security and added a special presidential suite near the entrance where chief executives could be taken.

Mr Trump was dispatched there briefly after the incident on Saturday night, local time.

# Tasmanian coastal community of Dolphin Sands on bushfire watch again

- [Topic:Bushfires](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/bushfires)

21h ago21 hours agoSat 25 Apr 2026 at 10:58pm

![Bushfire smoke plume seen from a backyard.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/da7f169149774de93ae0fa1de8b2f222?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=980&cropW=980&xPos=0&yPos=382&width=862&height=862)

The Dolphin Sands bushfire smoke plume seen from a backyard. (Supplied: Norman Bresler)

A coastal community recently ravaged by a bushfire has again been on alert in Tasmania.

Earlier on Sunday,the Tasmania Fire Service issued a Watch and Act for Dolphin Sands, on Tasmania's east coast, urging residents to monitor conditions as they are changing.

"There is a fire at McNeills Road, Swansea," the official advice read, adding that the fire "is not expected to cross the highway at this stage".

At 3:09 pm, the TFS downgraded the alert level, saying "there is no immediate threat".

Crews from the Tasmania Fire Serviceattended the fire.

The [Dolphin Sands community was hit by fire in December 2025](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-12-05/bushfire-dolphin-sands-tasmania-december-2025/106104816), with 19 homes destroyed.

People in the affected area have been urged to stay up to date with the latest advice via [ABC Local Radio](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/live/hobart) or by visiting [tasalert.com](http://tasalert.com/)

![Emergency vehicles and bushfire smoke plume.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c9db917eea370a266217c945ac5e0b2f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1775&cropW=2367&xPos=331&yPos=0&width=862&height=647)

People in the affected area are urged to stay up to date with the latest advice. (Supplied: Jessica Ross)

# Trump is political kryptonite in Australia but diplomacy still guides the way

By [Patricia Karvelas](https://www.abc.net.au/news/patricia-karvelas/6086082)

- [Topic:Foreign Affairs](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/foreign-affairs)

5h ago5 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 2:30pm

![Anthony Albanese takes a selfie with a smiling Donald Trump](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2cd5232e088ec6df609efda27359fb75?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=344&cropW=611&xPos=23&yPos=66&width=862&height=485)

Anthony Albanese must walk a careful line: keeping voters convinced of the merits of the US alliance, while not giving the impression he is being too reverential to Donald Trump. (Instagram: @Albomp)

The annual dinner for White House journalists has become the latest scene in the cycle of political violence we have come to expect from the country that is our greatest ally, with armed security rushing the event.

Attendees were mid-meal when a member of the White House security detail appeared on the ballroom floor and yelled for everyone to get down, with Secret Service agents shouting "shots fired", "stay down", and "out of the way, sir!" as US President Donald Trump and cabinet members were rushed out.

The cancer of political extremism and violence continues to infect the US, and Australians look across the Pacific with worry about the state of the world and the democratic health of a country we need and rely on to remain a healthy democracy.

## Political kryptonite in Australia

The news of Trump surviving [yet another brush](https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2026/04/25/trump-threats-assassination-attempts/89804208007/) with an armed shooter comes as new figures reveal just how contentious he is here.

A staggering 66 per cent of Australians disapprove of the way Trump is doing his job, up from 51 per cent disapproval late last year, with the controversial leader political kryptonite in Australia.

Polling by Talbot Mills Research, one of the research firms used by Labor, found the US president has dramatically lost support in Australia. Approval of Trump has fallen to 27 per cent, from 40 per cent in October last year.

The survey of more than 1,000 Australians taken this month, during the midst of the US/Israeli conflict with Iran, shows an increasing hostility to Trump.

The risk that his unpopularity starts infecting the Australian government should be a real concern for Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, who has been locked into ensuring our alliance with the US is maintained.

The government believes it has never been more important for Australia's long-term interests to stay close to the US, but also recognises that being besties with Trump risks covering themselves in electoral repellent.

Albanese needs to walk a careful line in how he keeps voters convinced of the merits of the alliance, while not giving the impression he is being too reverential to Trump.

![Donald Trump points his finger at a colleague, he is sitting and dressed in a navy suit.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/078e146bb1a66e56a91227afdedef097?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

In Australia, approval of Trump has fallen to 27 per cent, from 40 per cent in October last year. (Reuters: Kylie Cooper)

## Wide-spread anxiety in the community

When asked about the world becoming a much more unstable and dangerous place, 65 per cent of those surveyed said they were concerned about the issue — up from 59 per cent in January.

Those figures reflect the genuine and widespread community anxiety that has been bubbling away since the pandemic, with a sense that life has never really returned to normal and new and complex problems keep turning up at our door.

The war in Iran has not only resulted in fresh violence in the Middle East, but what started with worries about our fuel reserves is now quickly turning into broader angst about our supply chain.

Albanese has been effective in working with our regional neighbours to secure deals for more fuel, fertiliser and other essential products, but it is not lost on many that the reason we are in this tight squeeze is because the Iran War has shut down the critical shipping passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

The Australian government deployed a Wedgetail aircraft, defence personnel and missiles to support the US in the conflict, back in March, and was deliberate and repeated in framing its role as defensive and about protecting Australians in the region living in countries like the UAE.

The government has been firm on that nuance, aware that the distinction between offering support and engaging in offensive operations that look like we are following the US into another war risks not just community concern, but also a backlash from those in Labor's left.

## Leading with diplomacy

Against this backdrop, Albanese has reaffirmed his willingness to host Trump in Australia. The PM has done so despite the fractures emerging in the relationship as a result of the Iran conflict, including Trump being critical of Australia for not doing enough.

When asked if he had spoken to Trump, the prime minister confirmed he had spoken to him since the conflict started and described the exchange as constructive.

He also confirmed Australia remains in regular contact with the Trump administration.

"I had the conversation during this conflict with President Trump. It was a good conversation, it was a constructive conversation, as all of mine have been with him," Albanese told the ABC.

That diplomacy comes despite Trump publicly expressing dissatisfaction with Australia's contribution in the Middle East.

Albanese, for his part, is continuing to play a straight bat, with the standing invitation for Trump to visit Australia unchanged.

"Of course it is open … he knows where Australia is," the prime minister said.

"An American president would always be welcome in Australia, including President Trump. He would [always be welcome here](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-23/albanese-on-us-ties-amid-war-in-iran/106598696), as would any American president."

The prime minister's remarks signal an effort to maintain diplomatic stability as the conflict in the region drags on and gets more complex.

Albanese also backed moves to extend the ceasefire between the United States and Iran, reiterating calls for an end to the conflict.

"I just hope \[the end\] is as soon as possible," he said.

"We have called for de-escalation … I am absolutely happy with \[the ceasefire extension and\] anything that is about de-escalation, because quite clearly what I want to see is an end to the human impact, of course that it's having as well, but the economic impact."

## Domestic political fallout

That Albanese talks about the economic impact in the context of the conflict is no mistake. It has already been seized on and weaponised at home.

Advocacy group GetUp has raised more than $400,000 to target Pauline Hanson for her affinity to Trump in the battleground electorate of Farrer.

Binding Hanson with Trump, the campaign seeks to lay blame on him and by default her for the rising costs hitting regional voters, particularly fuel and fertiliser.

The populist party has staked its ambitions on candidate David Farley to win its first federal lower house contest — and become the party's second MP after a rapid surge in support over the summer.

GetUp's ads — plastered across billboards, television, radio and digital media — aim to dash these hopes by highlighting Hanson's links to Mr Trump and mining billionaire Gina Rinehart.

The fact that the Iran conflict has spilled into an issue playing out in the key Farrer by-election, to be held on May 9, highlights the stakes for Albanese.

It isn't just managing the alliance with the US, but also the force currently occupying the Oval Office.

**Patricia Karvelas is host of ABC News Afternoon Briefing at 4pm weekdays on ABC News Channel, co-host of the weekly Party Room podcast with Fran Kelly and host of politics and news podcast Politics Now.**

# Budget is in the dark as Hormuz oil crisis upends economic outlook

By [Alan Kohler](https://www.abc.net.au/news/alan-kohler/167112)

- [Topic:Budget](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/budget)

2h ago2 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 2:30pm

![A man in a suit from a side profile with his chin jutted out defiantly.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c1c7302a6849b1174a8dbbe44b7bfe01?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=900&cropW=1600&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

If the Strait of Hormuz is still closed by June, policymakers like Treasurer Jim Chalmers will face tough decisions. (ABC News: Luke Stephenson)

It looks like the 2026 federal budget will have to be prepared over the next fortnight with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, and the global economy in flux.

At least Treasury will be able to assume that the biggest single budget problem, the NDIS, has been fixed.

That's the benefit of pre-budget announcements like last week's proposed NDIS reforms: Treasury modellers can do the forward estimates on the basis that 160,000 people will no longer be on the NDIS, and the cost will be $15 billion a year less than it was when they last ran the numbers five months ago.

Whether health minister Mark Butler and NDIS minister Jenny McAllister can actually pull that off is neither here nor there — it's announced, therefore it is, to [paraphrase Rene Descartes](https://humanidades.com/en/i-think-therefore-i-am/).

That can't be said of Donald Trump's announcement on Truth Social over the weekend that the United States has " [total control](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/apr/23/trump-claims-us-has-total-control-over-strait-of-hormuz-as-iran-seizes-two-container-ships) over the Strait of Hormuz."

Maybe they do, maybe they don't, but so does Iran.

We seem to have entered a sort of hostage/siege phase of this war, where Iran has taken the Strait of Hormuz hostage, and the United States has besieged Iran, or at least its ports. "It is " [Sealed up Tight](https://www.bbc.com/audio/play/w1730c69yn9x0b7)," posted President Trump, "until such time as Iran is able to make a DEAL!!!"

The problem is that neither combatant is sufficiently hurting the other for a deal to be urgent enough for the rest of us.

## The big question

Iran's closure of the strait is not hurting the US directly at all. As Trump himself said: "The United States [imports almost no oil](https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R48903) through the Hormuz Strait and won't be taking any in the future. We don't need it. We haven't needed it, and we don't need it."

Sieges only work, or don't take a long time, if the enemy can be starved. America's siege of Iran's ports is painful, but Iran won't starve.

Trump says Iran is losing $US500 million a day ($700 million) as a result of the blockade, but that is a big exaggeration.

Before the war, Iran was selling 1.6 million barrels of oil a day for about $US65 a barrel, or a total of about $US100 million. Even at the current price, it would be only about $US150 million, but that's hypothetical, and not what they're "losing", and while the lack of imports is also hurting, the total cost is nowhere near $US500 million per day.

Not that Iran can last forever, but an economic analyst and professor at Shahid Beheshti University in Tehran, Saeed Laylaz, [told Agence France-Presse](https://www.france24.com/en/live-news/20260423-iran-economy-looks-set-to-withstand-us-naval-blockade) last week that "If the blockade lasts for more than two or three months, it can cause more damage". He didn't say Iran can't survive more than two or three months, but only that it will cause "more damage".

On the other hand, two or three months without any exports from the Persian Gulf would cause a great deal of damage to the global economy, probably a recession.

It's not just oil and therefore the diesel and jet fuel needed for logistics and air travel, which is bad enough, but the price of plastic packaging is now increasing rapidly because naphtha is in short supply, and food prices are rising because of a collapse in the supply of urea fertilisers.

So the question for the treasurer, Jim Chalmers, and the treasury secretary, Jenny Wilkinson, is: if the Strait of Hormuz is still closed on May 12, when does the budget assume it will reopen?

## A budget that doesn't have the numbers

Trump said he would send his son-in-law, Jared Kushner, and a real estate developer named Steve Witkoff, back to the Serena Hotel in Islamabad to resume negotiations, but when the Iranians refused to negotiate while under blockade, he cancelled their trip. So with the clock ticking of the budget, there are no talks going on at all.

Maybe they will turn up and negotiate, and maybe one side, or both, will fold within two weeks, but that seems very unlikely.

Much more likely is that on budget night, Treasury boffins will still have no idea what numbers to plug into their budget model for the oil price, gas exports, plastic and food prices, inflation, nominal GDP, and tax revenue.

In other words, this year's budget will be a lot worse than "uncertain", as Chalmers has been warning — it will basically be irrelevant, a sort of holding document pending another budget once the status of exports out of the Persian Gulf is settled.

With the hardline Revolutionary Guard Corps now in charge in Iran, and with power apparently split among 31 regional commanders, getting some kind of quick capitulation from Iran seems out of the question.

The most likely scenario is that Trump declares victory at some point and goes home, probably with the same deal that was signed in 2015, the one known as the JCPOA, Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action.

## And a ceasefire without peace or stability

Iran would end up like the Korean Peninsula after the Korean War — that is, a ceasefire without peace. Like North Korea, Iran would go flat out to develop a nuclear bomb.

That would probably encourage others in the region to do the same, like Turkey and Saudi Arabia.

The Middle East would become an even more dangerous and volatile mess, with other Persian Gulf countries forced to pay Iran's toll to export and import through the Strait of Hormuz. Global shipping would be destabilised, and the oil price would stay high.

The only scenario that would not mean higher inflation and lower economic growth — stagflation — is one in which the IRGC, the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, quickly loses its grip on power and Iran's military dictatorship ends. Right now, that scenario looks the least likely.

## The future is complex

Accompanying this oil shock (and gas, plastics and fertiliser shock) is a profound change in the way fiscal and monetary policymakers operate.

Recessions used to be seen as "cleansing" events, necessary to reset the economy and control inflation.

In 1979, Fed chairman Paul Volcker did not hesitate to crush the US and world economies to remove the inflation that had become embedded after two oil shocks in the 1970s. And Paul Keating will always be remembered for telling us in November 1990 that the recession, then underway, was the one we had to have.

The Global Financial Crisis changed all that. The 2008 "great recession" (except in Australia) was caused by a collapse in demand, in turn caused by a financial crisis, not the sort of supply shortage of the '70s or the terms of trade crisis of the late-'80s, so policymakers agreed that demand had to be supported in 2008/9, not retrenched.

Budget deficits exploded and interest rates were slashed, but after the crisis, government debt was not reduced and interest rates were not put back where they were, and in fact, when inflation stayed low after 2013, rates were cut below what they had been in the GFC.

The COVID pandemic saw a collapse in both supply and demand, but policymakers focused on boosting demand, cutting interest rates to zero and below zero, and pumping up fiscal support again.

That rejection of economic rationalism has since been reinforced by the rise of political populism, both left and right, as technocratic, administrative political classes try to hold onto power with fiscal generosity, while those countries that have succumbed to populism — like the United States — do the same, except worse.

It's probably going too far to say that recessions have been abolished by political and economic elites, but central banks and governments are much keener to avoid them now than they were 30 years ago.

So if the Strait of Hormuz is still closed as April turns into May and then, God forbid, June and beyond, policymakers will have a tough decision.

They will face the clearest supply-side stagflation since the 1970s, and boosting demand with rate cuts and fiscal stimulus as they did during the GFC and pandemic would only make the inflation, and the eventual reckoning, worse.

Jim Chalmers might have to preside over another recession we have to have.

**Alan Kohler is a finance presenter and columnist on ABC News, and he also writes for Intelligent Investor.**

# Qld Premier David Crisafulli attends Anzac Day event with no Acknowledgement of Country

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c579014e7f3dca92fd95ca93e96f2257?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3054&cropW=3054&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
L\\
\\
By Liana Walker](https://www.abc.net.au/news/liana-walker/14012164)
- [Topic:Anzac Day](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/anzac-day)

8h ago8 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 12:17am

![David Crisafulli speaking at a lecturn.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c7f2c45d290794c201b00119d42fa9f5?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1557&cropW=2768&xPos=0&yPos=145&width=862&height=485)

Queensland Premier David Crisafulli did not include an Acknowledgement of Country in his Anzac Day speech. (ABC News: Baz Ruddick)

## In short:

Queensland Premier David Crisafulli has attended an Anzac Day ceremony in Townsville that did not include a Welcome to or Acknowledgement of Country.

It comes as events in Melbourne, Sydney and Perth experienced booing and heckling during Welcome to Country at Dawn Services.

## What's next?

Federal Opposition Leader Angus Taylor denounced booing at Anzac Day services, but said he would like to see Welcome to Country used less.

The Queensland premier has attended a dawn service in Townsville on Anzac Day, which did not include an Acknowledgement of Country, as southern states experienced heckling and booing during their services.

The event in Townsville, which has a proud military history and is Australia's largest garrison city, also did not include a Welcome to Country.

Premier David Crisafulli and Townsville Mayor Nick Dametto attended the ceremony in Anzac Memorial Park held by the Townsville RSL sub-branch.

President of the Townsville RSL sub-branch Colin Mosch confirmed the branch had decided not to include Welcome to or Acknowledgement of Country in the Anzac Day Dawn Service.

> "The Anzac Day Dawn Service is about one thing, remembering those who have served and have paid the ultimate sacrifice,"

Mr Mosch said.

![A soldier looking down in low light. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/9b7ee004f5562c1771db27cff1c7d152?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1845&cropW=2768&xPos=0&yPos=1&width=862&height=575)

The service was organised by the Townsville RSL sub-branch.  (ABC News: Baz Ruddick)

The 2025 and 2024 services in Townsville both included an Acknowledgement of Country for the traditional landholders, the Bindal and Wulgurukaba peoples.

The branch confirmed it was responsible for coordinating the protocol for Anzac Day services, not the Townsville City Council, which assisted the branch in delivering the events.

Outside of Queensland, [booing and heckling occurred during Welcome to Country at dawn services](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-25/anzac-day-booing-heckling-melbourne-sydney-condemnation/106605674) around the country including in Melbourne, Sydney and Perth.

Mr Crisafulli's office did not respond to a request for comment.

![A cenotaph in low light. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/5349a0dafe1bd05921d02d2ec0febbd0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2827&cropW=4240&xPos=0&yPos=5&width=862&height=575)

The dawn service was held at Anzac Memorial Park.  (ABC News: Baz Ruddick)

Police Minister Dan Purdie said Anzac Day was the "most important day on our calendar" and he attended six Anzac Day events.

In response to the premier's attendance at the Townsville event, Mr Purdie said the events were organised by local schools and RSLs.

"We don't organise these events, we say yes to as many as we possibly can and we attend," he said.

## Indigenous commissioner labels attendance 'disrespectful'

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Social Justice Commissioner Katie Kiss said there were a lot of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander servicemen who fought in World War I.

"I think having the premier attend those events and not give recognition to our black diggers, I think, is very disrespectful," she said.

"I also note that Palm Island, which was one of the major reserves in the Queensland jurisdiction where Aboriginal people were sent under the Protection Act, is just across the way and they held a beautiful ceremony yesterday to pay respect to all those who served in the wars in Australia."

![Katie Kiss with a neutral expression. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/9ed0c6dcec369465d7903d4900bfd758?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=29&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Katie Kiss says many First Nations people fought in World War I.  (ABC News)

In response to the premier attending the Townsville ceremony, Opposition Leader Steven Miles said Anzac Day programs were up to the RSL in each community.

"It's a day that we mark respect for all who served, including First Nations Australians," he said.

Leader of the federal Opposition Angus Taylor said booing during Anzac Day ceremonies, whatever the cause, was "inappropriate and un-Australian".

"I can understand the frustration Australians feel about overuse of Welcome to Country.

"I feel that at times, often actually, I think it is overused," he told Insiders.

"I would like to see them used less and therefore not devalued as I think they have been over time."

# Man expects to nab Guinness World Record after running half-marathon on stilts

- [By Laura Mayers](https://www.abc.net.au/news/laura-mayers/12950738)
- [ABC Ballarat](https://www.abc.net.au/ballarat/)
- [Topic:Marathon](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/marathon)

20h ago20 hours agoSat 25 Apr 2026 at 11:03pm

![A man in stilts with a flag](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2c6158e2a5bd35a4f660f06f4e484eba?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2583&cropW=4592&xPos=0&yPos=432&width=862&height=485)

Colombian man Santiago Vergara ran the Ballarat half-marathon on 90-centimetre-tall stilts. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

## In short:

A man has run the Ballarat half-marathon on 90-centimetre stilts in 2 hours and 34 minutes.

Santiago Vergara's run in Australia's fourth-largest marathon was a tall tilt at a new Guinness World Record.

Mr Vergara says officials at Guinness World Records told him he would have to beat a current record of 3 hours.

Santiago Vergara quite literally views the world from on high.

A performer and artist, the Colombian man took to the Ballarat half-marathon today to attempt quite a feat.

Mr Vergara ran the 21-kilometre race on 90-centimetre stilts.

While hundreds of runners took to the streets of Ballarat, abuzz with excitement over their own athletic feats, many stopped in their stretching and limbering up just minutes before the run to take a selfie with Mr Vergara.

Running the event in just 2 hours and 34 minutes, the 33-year-old said the stilt stunt might have earned him a Guinness World Record.

![A man stands much taller than the crowd around him](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4a5527871f1a6758053701840118867b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=150&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Santiago Vergara's attempt to nab a Guinness World Record was met with cheers and high fives from other runners today. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

> "The Guinness World Records told me I needed to do it in less than 3 hours and 5 minutes, now I'm going to have to send them all the evidence … but in the official time, I did it."

Beaming and barely breaking a sweat after the 2.5-hour run on stilts, Mr Vergara said he was thrilled with the result.

"I'm exhausted, I'm tired, I'm really, really sore … but very, very happy," Mr Vergara said.

"I tried to follow the pace of \[others\] but at kilometre 15 they started to \[put in\] distance with me and \[others\].

> "The last part of the race was really difficult, and I was almost falling but I could do it — I tried to breathe, and I did 2 \[hours\] 34 minutes."

![A man holds hands with two people while on stilts](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/004a9e361f79015be7ba52b166c54b78?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=150&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Santiago Vergara ran the Ballarat half-marathon in 2 hours and 34 minutes. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

There were plenty of runners and onlookers alike who were stumped at the tall tilt for the Guinness World Record, with many stopping to ask Mr Vergara why he would undertake such a feat.

"I've been walking on stilts for 18 years now, and I love my stilts with all of my soul," he said.

"I'm looking for challenges all the time with my stilts, and not only that but I bring them everywhere.

"The first time I saw the sea, I brought my stilts … I've gone to Machu Picchu with my stilts … I've walked, and climbed mountains, I've done many beautiful things on them."

Mr Vergara was not the first person to run a marathon or half-marathon in stilts.

Michelle Frost ran the fastest marathon on stilts in 6 hours and 37 minutes in 2018 at the London marathon.

Brother and sister duo Charley Phillips and George Phillips held the title before, running the 2012 London Marathon in 6 hours and 50 minutes.

![Four people in medieval armour pose](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/28b5c592d7156809cdaa3d5d99d778ed?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3061&cropW=4592&xPos=0&yPos=193&width=862&height=575)

Phillip Leitch, Renae Marimsa, Samson Miller and Alex Gay were a sight to behold during the 5km run on Saturday. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

## Knights of the round marathon circuit

Mr Vergara wasn't the only adventurous athlete who was gaining attention at the two-day marathon event over the weekend.

In shining medieval metal armour, Phillip Leitch, Renae Marimsa, Samson Miller and Alex Gay were a sight to behold during the 5km run on Saturday.

Despite warm weather and sunny skies, Mr Leitch and his Knights completed the course in under an hour.

"I'm feeling good, everyone pulled up really well. We were a bit surprised, our goal was under an hour — and we managed to keep moving the entire time, no stops," Mr Leitch said.

The four Knights are performers and jousters from Ballarat's medieval theme park, Kryal Castle.

Mr Leitch said they thought they would attempt the feat again next year.

![A man in a full suit of armour looks at the camera](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/de7a0249d4f7e7e8fc994fb7692942fe?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3061&cropW=4592&xPos=0&yPos=193&width=862&height=575)

Phillip Leitch is a performer and athlete from Kryal Castle, who says running in armour has helped his fitness levels. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

"I imagine it'll be a regular thing for us, there are other knights at the Kryal Castle who saw us do it and might be keen to join, now," he said.

> "The amount of support we had from people going down the road, people congratulating us after, it might be a silly thing to do but people really enjoy it."

Not one to let moss gather on a steel breastplate, Mr Leitch was back to work at Kryal Castle again today, in his armour to battle it out in a jousting tournament.

"There was quite the recovery … but jousting event today, all back to work, situation-normal. The fitness we achieved through training will help us with our jousting, our sport, in the long-run."

![A man cheers as he runs on stilts](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e85961a3fb82698b95005c1a01910180?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=150&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Santiago Vergara says he was very tired and sore after the 21-kilometre run on stilts. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

Organiser of the Ballarat Marathon Adam McNichol said when Mr Vergara rang to ask if the stilt stunt would be possible, he was not completely surprised.

"I have to say, in the running world, part of the boom in these events is that it's for everyone — from elite athletes, to people with crazy events," Mr McNichol said.

"We absolutely encourage this kind of thing.

"We have to make sure, but it's heaps of fun. We want our event to be super professional, but super fun. We have half a dozen Olympians running, but then we have our man on stilts.

"We are happy for our event to be put on the map in any weird and wonderful way possible."

# Victorian government offers 20pc car registration discount in cost-of-living measure

- [Topic:Cost of Living](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/cost-of-living)

10h ago10 hours agoSat 25 Apr 2026 at 9:33pm

![A licence plate](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/bfae6da883bfbd1dd54a52c81c905fcf?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=949&cropW=1687&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

The temporary cut to registration fees in Victoria will save a driver $186.

(ABC News: Danielle Bonica)

Victorian motorists will be able to claim back 20 per cent of their car registration fees as part of a cost-of-living measure from the government.

From June 1, eligible vehicle owners will be able to apply for a rebate on the registration fees paid for the 2025/26 year through the Service Victoria website.

Registration for a single car currently costs $930.70 a year, with the discount meaning a driver will receive $186 back.

Victorians will be eligible to claim for up to two cars in their name, but the rebate will only be available until July 31.

The rebate comes after the government announced public transport discounts across the state as war in the Middle East continues to put pressure on fuel prices.

Public transport will remain free until the end of May and then be half-price for the rest of the year.

![Jacinta Allan speaks at a press conference.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ecd6a26ad180ffd8ddb42d4e81aa0ee2?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=150&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Jacinta Allan says the cost-of-living measure is necessary as the war in Iran pushes up prices. (ABC News)

Premier Jacinta Allan said it would "provide some relief at a time when working people and their families are looking for this support from their government".

“I do want to acknowledge that this won't solve every problem but it is another step we can take,” Ms Allan said on Sunday.

The car registration rebate is expected to cost the government about $750 million in foregone revenue.

Ms Allan said the blow to the state's coffers would be accounted for in the upcoming budget.

"We can afford it because it's one-off cost-of-living help right now, while we are delivering a surplus," she said.

"Like cheaper public transport, this won't fix everything, but it's immediate action I can take to make a difference."

The opposition has criticised the rollout of the rebate, saying the short eligibility period and need to apply would mean many Victorians miss out.

"You’ve got to find the form, fill in the form, have the form processed," opposition spokesperson James Newbury said.

"The government could give you the money today. They don’t want to do that because they don’t want to give you the money, they want the headline."

St Vincent de Paul Society Victoria chief executive Charlie Spendlove said while the initiative would provide temporary relief, more needed to be done to provide long-term cost-of-living relief for families..

"This concession is going to make a big difference right now for those living on the poverty line, living day-to-day, moment-to-moment," she said.

"But what we really need the government to look at is long-term solutions, including affordable and community housing."

# From gangsters' paradise to police sting. How Istanbul turned on Australia's fugitives

Exclusive by [Anton Rose](https://www.abc.net.au/news/anton-rose/105260824)

- [Topic:Crime](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/crime)

3h ago3 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 4:29pm

![A graphic of a bearded man in grainy black-and-white against a red backdrop of the Turkish flag and Istanbul skyline.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/1ed8fdf05a1cffac9f56eb023299be25?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=745&cropW=1324&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Masood Zakaria's deportation from Türkiye was akin to "a kidnapping" by the AFP, according to his lawyers. (ABC News: Sharon Gordon)

A secret series of police intelligence reports and internal emails have unravelled the inner workings of Australia's most wanted alleged criminals on the run in Istanbul.

The documents offer a never-before-seen insight into some of the most notorious underworld figures believed by police to be running Sydney's drug trade from overseas.

They have emerged as part of a wide-ranging subpoena by alleged crime boss Masood Zakaria to police, who is arguing his 2023 deportation to Australia from Türkiye was illegal.

In the space of two years, Mr Zakaria went from a maritime stowaway fugitive to one of the highest-profile Australian Federal Police (AFP) captures in recent memory.

![A handcuffed man with police holding each of his elbows in what appears to be an airport terminal.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ac6a0e951599059fae64247160370f86?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=150&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Masood Zakaria's return to Australia was seen as a win for the AFP. How he was deported is now facing scrutiny. (ABC News)

The alleged Sydney crime boss is believed to have made an audacious escape from Australia in December 2021.

The most consistent theory outlined by police in the documents is that Mr Zakaria was able to flee the country for Asia on a fishing vessel from either Queensland or Western Australia.

A few days before he fled, arrest warrants had been issued charging the man police claim is a senior figure within the Alameddine crime family with two counts of murder.

![Salim and Toufik Hamze](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/41218e1fcb3a6dafff7b445e2268c31d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=876&cropW=1314&xPos=63&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Salim Hamze, 18, (right) and father Toufik Hamze who were gunned down in Guildford in 2021. (Supplied)

The warrants were for [the fatal shooting of Salim and Toufik Hamze at the height of the gangland war between the Alameddine and Hamzy families in 2021](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2021-10-20/sydney-gangland-shooting-leaves-father-and-son-dead/100552710).

Mr Zakaria will fight those charges at trial.

What followed next, his year on the run, has been subject to speculation in both law enforcement and underworld circles.

But the ABC can now chart how Mr Zakaria was reportedly spotted in Jordan and allegedly mingled with organised crime figures in Istanbul before his sensational arrest in a seaside villa.

Mr Zakaria, now back in Australia, is fighting to have his charges permanently stayed, arguing his deportation back to Sydney was akin to "a kidnapping" by the AFP.

## The 'Aussie Cartel'

![A shirtless, powerfully built man with tattooes pouts for the camera.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d30d0d108c634b0c8218e41f466497a0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=411&cropW=617&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Hakan Ayik, while in Türkiye, became Australia's most wanted man after an AFP sting unmasked him as a drug importing mastermind.  (Supplied)

Istanbul has long served as a base for some of Australia's most notorious criminals.

Hakan Ayik, his cousin Hakan "Little Hux" Arif and Melbourne bikie Hasan Topal were established members of what has been dubbed the "Aussie Cartel" based in the Turkish city, some for more than a decade.

The trio have been accused by Turkish police of major drug importations to Australia from their hideaway after emerging from local chapters of the powerful Comanchero bikie gang.

![A shirtless, tattooed man in a gym.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/504875d6d1a549f5dc269a460d4a8fc4?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=627&cropW=940&xPos=0&yPos=115&width=862&height=575)

Alleged drug lord Ayik spent more than a decade on the run before he was apprehended. (Facebook)

Police documents said the "Aussie Cartel" had been running a billion-dollar global drug empire from overseas, controlling the narcotics trade in Australia and beyond.

Once Australia's most wanted man, Ayik gained notoriety as the "Facebook bikie" after posting glamorous pictures while on the run from Australian law enforcement.

That was before the [AFP duped him into supplying police-bugged phones to the underworld and his subsequent arrest by Turkish police](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-07-12/nsw-most-wanted-man-former-sydney-bikie-hakan-ayik/105518070) on drugs and money laundering offences.

His cousin Hakan Arif is a former Comanchero member also arrested by Turkish police, accused of overseeing drug shipments across the world.

![A middle-aged man on his knees in a room with three masked men, two in military gear and holding automatic rifles. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/918561fb0c92414ca291f9fb52e7214e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=576&cropW=864&xPos=80&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Turkish police arresting Sydney organised crime figure Hakan Arif in Istanbul in 2023.

In 2017, he avoided extradition back to Australia from Dubai on drugs charges on a legal technicality.

Arif was also netted in the same Turkish police operation that took down Ayik in 2023.

Both have been accused by law enforcement of being central figures in the "Aussie Cartel".

## A summer in Bodrum

Istanbul had become the favoured hideout of Australian criminals due to Türkiye's policy of not extraditing its own nationals and its straightforward path to citizenship.

Not long after Mr Zakaria arrived in the country, intelligence began making its way to the powerful NSW Crime Commission.

He has not been accused of committing any crimes while in Türkiye.

![Landscape shot showing the the town of Bodrum and the ocean ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/23b35bc2783bd1996bdf44d9a3f41e0e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2000&cropW=3000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The seaside village of Bodrum, where Mr Zakaria and other Australian crime figures spent the summer of 2022. (Unsplash: Mert Kahveci)

According to one information report from the agency filed to the Supreme Court as part of the challenge to his extradition, Mr Zakaria was spotted in the company of the "Aussie Cartel".

The information was categorised as "usually reliable" and "possibly true".

> "Masood Zakaria was observed in Turkey in the company of Little Hux \[Arif\]. It is assumed the sighting of Masood Zakaria and Hakan Arif occurred recently in Istanbul," it stated.
>
> "A number of Australian organised crime identities will travel to Bodrum for the Turkish summer. It is assumed that this will include Masood Zakaria and Hakan Arif."

One source, who requested anonymity, told the ABC the group and Mr Zakaria were often seen inside Ayik's Kings Cross Hotel watching UFC fights.

## Money laundering scam and the 'Merrylands crew'

One bizarre tale to emerge from Mr Zakaria's pursuit of documents in the Supreme Court centres around Hakan Arif, who fled Australia in 2014.

In documents that emerged from the legal challenge, police intelligence showed that it was likely Hakan Arif led Turkish police to Mr Zakaria.

In one information report from the NSW Crime Commission, it sources reported Mr Zakaria had come into contact with the man nicknamed "Little Hux".

![A headshot of a man quietly smiling.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d4d00924ea6081ebbd46ede0258100d1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=529&cropW=793&xPos=0&yPos=15&width=862&height=575)

Sydney underworld figure Murat Gulasi, who died inside an Istanbul gym while spending time with other Australian gangsters in Türkiye. (Supplied)

The document filed by the agency in 2022 detailed how Mr Zakaria recovered a sum of money for his friend Murat Gulasi from Hakan Arif.

> "Later in 2022, Hakan Arif claimed to have access to a method to 'clean' the proceeds of crime in Turkey and offered Masood Zakaria the opportunity to employ the method," the document stated.
>
> "Masood Zakaria provided Hakan Arif with \[redacted\] and the 'Merrylands crew' provided Hakan Arif with a further \[redacted\]."

References to the Merrylands crew is likely Sydney's feared Alameddine crime family, led by exiled gangster Rafat Alameddine in Lebanon, the report claimed.

"Hakan Arif did not have access to any such method to launder the proceeds of crime but created the story as a means to gain access to, and steal, the funds," the report said.

The same document stated Mr Arif: "Then provided information to Turkish authorities that resulted in Masood Zakaria's arrest and subsequent detention in Turkey."

Mr Zakaria and 17 other Australians, including Sydney lawyer Sylvan Singh, were apprehended in the village of Mugla by local police, according to court documents.

Mr Singh survived a shooting in May 2025 that claimed the life of Mr Zakaria's brother Dawood at Granville.

![Head-and-shoulder shot of a young bearded man in suit and tie.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/61ef432d73ee16943eda58c0edc9ef57?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=517&cropW=776&xPos=0&yPos=39&width=862&height=575)

Sydney solicitor Sylvan Singh, who in police documents was named as an allege member of the gang "KVT". (Supplied)

The solicitor was later charged with allegedly kidnapping and torturing a man and large-scale drug supply offences.

He denies all allegations.

The tranche of emails revealed in court detail how police considered Mr Singh a member of the KVT — a Western Sydney gang consisting of Fijian Australians.

At Mr Singh's previous bail hearing last year, a court heard how he was "removed" from Türkiye after his visa was cancelled the same day Mr Zakaria was apprehended there.

## The escape and the capture

Two days before Mr Zakaria was set to be arrested by the NSW Police on December 15, 2021, he vanished.

What happened has never been confirmed, but internally, NSW Police had a theory.

Mr Zakaria was one of several alleged Alameddine crime figures who avoided capture that day in what police were anticipating would be a series of "large scale arrests" targeting the network.

![An elevated view across a roundabout with red-roofed white homes stretching towards low hills.  ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ca62b80556179baef02977bc6dee9c5f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=568&cropW=852&xPos=48&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The quiet Turkish town of Mugla, where Masood Zakaria and more than a dozen Australians were apprehended by police in early 2023. (Wikimedia Commons: [Alisan Senyuz](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Zihni_Derin_Street_Mugla_Turkey.jpg))

The Alameddines, according to an email by the force's head of Organised Crime Directorate, Grant Taylor, tendered as part of Mr Zakaria's subpoena of law enforcement, had help from "trusted insiders".

"It is now clear that Zakaria was provided sensitive information about his impending arrest by corrupt official/s," he wrote to an AFP colleague.

Stowing away on what police believe was a fishing vessel, Mr Zakaria crossed into Türkiye by land on an Afghani passport months later.

Another report from the NSW Crime Commission unearthed in Mr Zakaria's subpoena detailed how he was spotted in Jordan with the wife and child of another Australian crime figure.

Eventually captured with a dozen other Alameddine figures in Mugla in January 2023, the documents show the frantic efforts to get him home — an extradition now facing scrutiny by the court system.

"They're trying to collect money from associates to pay off the police," one AFP officer wrote in an email.

"Are you able to confirm if Zakaria has been arrested? Thought it might be worth making inquiries with the Turks to avoid anything shonky being done by the locals."
